title: Listening - Part 3: The Power of the Past
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a psychologist, Abbi, and a marketing expert, Elliot, about why humans are so obsessed with the past. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring nostalgia as a 'psychological resource,' its use in advertising, and the 'rosy retrospection' bias.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We live in a world of 'reboots' and 'retro' fashion. Is nostalgia just a way to escape the present? Joining me are Dr. Abbi, a psychologist, and Elliot, a brand strategist. Abbi, is nostalgia a sign of weakness?

Abbi: [15] Historically, it was seen as a mental illness—a form of 'melancholy.' But today, we see it as a powerful 'psychological resource.' [/15] When we face transition or stress, looking back at a stable past helps us feel more 'grounded.' it’s a way of maintaining a sense of 'self-continuity.' It reminds us who we are when the world feels like it’s changing too fast.

Interviewer: Elliot, you use this in marketing. Why does a 'vintage' look sell so well?

Elliot: [16] Because nostalgia is an emotional 'shortcut.' It associates a new product with a time when the customer felt safe and happy. [/16] When we see a logo from our childhood, our brain releases dopamine. It’s not about the product; it’s about the feeling. That’s why you see Gen Z—who never even used a cassette tape—buying them. They are 'nostalgic' for a time they never even lived through.

Interviewer: Abbi, is our memory of the past actually accurate?

Abbi: [17] Almost never. We suffer from 'rosy retrospection.' Our brains are designed to filter out the boring or painful parts of the past and highlight the best bits. [/17] We remember the summer holidays, but we forget the mosquitoes and the long car rides. This 'curated' past makes the present seem much worse by comparison.

Interviewer: Elliot, is there a danger of 'nostalgia overload' in culture?

Elliot: [18] Yes. If we are only looking backward, we stop innovating. We’re seeing this in Hollywood—studios are terrified of new ideas, so they just remake the same five movies over and over. [/18] It’s a safe investment, but it leads to a 'cultural stagnation.' We risk becoming a society that only consumes its own history.

Interviewer: Abbi, can nostalgia ever be collective? Like a whole nation looking back?

Abbi: [19] It can, and that’s where it becomes political. It can be used to create a sense of unity, but also to exclude people who 'don't fit' that idealized version of the past. [/19] It’s often used to sell a vision of a 'golden age' that never really existed for everyone.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Elliot? How do we balance the past and the future?

Elliot: [20] The best brands use 'future-stalgia.' They take the emotional warmth of the past but combine it with modern technology and values. You have to move forward, but you can take your favorite memories with you. [/20]

Interviewer: Abbi, Elliot, thank you.

questions.question: Abbi says that today, psychologists view nostalgia as
options: A) a dangerous form of mental illness.
B) a tool that helps people deal with stress.
C) a sign that a person is unable to grow.
D) a way for people to ignore their current problems.
questions.explanation: She calls it a 'powerful psychological resource' that helps us feel 'grounded' during stress.

questions.question: Elliot explains that brands use nostalgia because it
options: A) is cheaper than creating a new brand identity.
B) allows them to sell low-quality products to young people.
C) creates an immediate emotional connection with the customer.
D) is the only way to attract customers from Gen Z.
questions.explanation: He says it's an 'emotional shortcut' that associates a product with a time when the customer felt safe and happy.

questions.question: The term 'rosy retrospection' refers to the brain's tendency to
options: A) remember every detail of a past event perfectly.
B) focus only on the positive aspects of past experiences.
C) confuse dreams with real memories from childhood.
D) become more depressed as a person gets older.
questions.explanation: Abbi says the brain filters out boring or painful parts and highlights the best bits.

questions.question: What is the 'cultural stagnation' that Elliot mentions?
options: A) A lack of interest in going to the cinema or theater.
B) A situation where no new creative ideas are being produced.
C) The destruction of old buildings to make way for new ones.
D) The increase in the price of vintage clothing and items.
questions.explanation: He relates it to Hollywood remaking old movies because they are 'terrified of new ideas'.

questions.question: Abbi warns that when nostalgia is used politically, it can
options: A) help all citizens feel equally included in a nation.
B) lead to a better understanding of historical facts.
C) be used to promote an unrealistic and exclusive vision of the past.
D) prevent a country from trading with other nations.
questions.explanation: She mentions it can be used to exclude people who don't fit an 'idealized version' of a 'golden age'.

questions.question: Elliot's concept of 'future-stalgia' involves
options: A) rejecting the past and focusing only on new technology.
B) combining emotional elements of the past with modern values.
C) creating products that look old but are actually very expensive.
D) writing books about what the world will look like in 100 years.
questions.explanation: He defines it as taking the 'emotional warmth of the past' and combining it with 'modern technology and values'.
